Project state and trade-offs — month 8

Headline: surface metrics are green but the project is absorbing pressure into quality and into team capacity that is not visible in the cost line. Detail below across the five variables.

Scope. Documented scope is unchanged. In practice, the non-core brand POS work has been quietly deferred — the team has prioritised core brands first, with non-core integration likely to slip into a phase 2 follow-up. This decision is not in any scope-change document. Recommend formalising as a deferred-scope item ahead of next steering. Sponsor's mental model also has elements not in the documented scope (loyalty-team morale, board narrative on digital transformation) — those are out of scope as documented but worth a conversation if they are influencing his expectations.

Schedule. Showing green. The 4-6 week POS integration overrun has been absorbed by team overtime (240 hours logged in the last six weeks, not billed to the project). Schedule contingency originally 3 weeks; in practice consumed already through this absorption, even though the project plan does not show it. If a further slip materialises, no contingency remains.

Cost. Showing green. The £150k cost contingency is officially untouched. In practice, the overtime above is a real labour cost that is being absorbed into the team's general capacity rather than billed. If we costed it conservatively at standard rates, that is roughly £18k of effort already absorbed.

Quality. Test coverage on non-core brand flows has been compressed to 62% from a planned 85%. The fallback authentication pattern (deployed because of the licensing issue) has been flagged by the security lead as a medium-term risk; not yet in the RAID register. Both are quality cuts, both are absorbing pressure that the other variables are not.

Risk. The RAID register shows 12 active risks (3 lower than start-of-quarter), but at least two new risks should be added: (a) post-launch defect risk on non-core brand flows due to compressed UAT, and (b) auth-fallback security risk with no documented remediation date. Recommend both go in this week.

Trade-offs that are currently being made implicitly:

- Schedule pressure is being absorbed into quality (compressed UAT) and into team capacity (overtime).
- Cost pressure is being deferred (overtime not billed; non-core POS deferred to phase 2).
- The "green status" is real on the visible variables but conceals real absorption on quality and on hidden cost.

Long-term implications, in rough magnitude:

- Compressed UAT on non-core flows: expect 1-3 post-launch defect spikes within first 60 days, remediation effort ~£50-150k depending on severity.
- Auth fallback: medium-term security risk; if it materialises before the planned remediation, incident response cost likely £100-500k.
- Non-core POS deferral: phase 2 work likely £200-400k separately funded, plus 2-4 months delay before full multi-brand functionality.

Recommendation to PM: surface these to sponsor in a private conversation ahead of next steering. The choice to absorb into quality and into team capacity is defensible if conscious; it is dangerous if it remains implicit.

— Coordinator
